questions: Does “retweet” mean reposting any arbitrary X status ID, or only reposting posts previously created by this bot/publication system?
What is the required scheduling input and timezone behavior for users: UTC ISO-8601 only, Discord timestamps, or natural-language times from admin chat?
Is the first release expected to ship only X/Twitter with an extensible architecture, or must at least one additional outbound platform be operational in this change?
Should per-channel routing be purely automatic from channel config, or can callers override the resolved route/platform set explicitly on a per-request basis?
What retry policy is desired for scheduled failures: no retry, fixed retry count, or backoff with manual intervention after exhaustion?

success_criteria.criterion: An immediate `share_to_social` request without `schedule_for` still publishes to X and returns a concrete success payload with `tweet_url` and `tweet_id`.
A scheduled publication can be created for `post`, `reply`, and `retweet`, and each job ends in a durable terminal state (`succeeded` or `failed`) with timestamps and error details.
Both admin-triggered and reaction-triggered publishing flow through the same unified execution service instead of separate direct-to-Twitter code paths.
Per-channel routing resolves using channel override, then parent channel, then guild default, while still honoring the existing `sharing_enabled` gate.
Successful X publishes still support the current delete/duplicate behavior through `shared_posts` compatibility or an equivalent migrated path.
Focused automated tests cover request normalization, routing resolution, queue claiming, and success/failure transitions, and those tests pass locally.
The platform integration boundary is provider-based so adding a second outbound platform does not require rewriting caller code.
The scheduler reuses the repo’s existing `discord.ext.tasks.loop` polling style instead of introducing APScheduler unless a concrete gap appears.
Worker logs include publication id, platform, action, and final provider error to make manual recovery straightforward.
Manual smoke tests on a real X account pass for immediate post, scheduled post, scheduled reply, and scheduled retweet.

assumptions: The first implementation only needs one live provider, X/Twitter, but the internal service and DB model should be platform-neutral.
A new canonical queue table is acceptable, and `shared_posts` can remain as a compatibility write-through during migration.
Scheduled publications will be stored and evaluated in UTC, with any user-facing timezone parsing normalized before persistence.
The bot runs as a single logical worker process most of the time, so DB-based claim/update semantics are sufficient to avoid duplicate execution.
Reaction-based sharing should continue to respect existing consent and moderation behavior; this project changes the execution backend, not the approval policy.
